main_actor: Romulus (legendary) / Latin-Sabine communities of the Tiber valley
description: The traditional founding date of Rome, 21 April 753 BCE (calculated by the antiquarian Varro in the 1st century BCE), marks the beginning of Roman chronological reckoning (ab urbe condita). Archaeological evidence from the Palatine Hill confirms Iron Age hut settlements dating to the mid-8th century BCE, broadly consistent with the traditional date, though the site was continuously inhabited from at least the 10th century BCE. The legendary narrative of Romulus and Remus (fratricide, asylum for fugitives, rape of the Sabine women) is mythological rather than historical, but the archaeological record supports the emergence of a proto-urban settlement consolidating multiple hilltop villages around the Forum valley in this period.
ethical_notes: The traditional founding narrative includes the rape of the Sabine women (raptio Sabinarum), which Roman sources treated as a legitimate act of state-building. This must not be euphemized: it describes the mass abduction and forced marriage of women. The legendary asylum (asylum Romuli) for fugitives and outcasts, while mythological, reflects an early Roman self-image of inclusive heterogeneity that contrasts with later exclusionary practices.

entity_links.notes: Traditional founding of Rome as a city; the Republic begins ~509 BCE

sources.citation: Cornell, T.J. (1995). The Beginnings of Rome: Italy and Rome from the Bronze Age to the Punic Wars (c. 1000-264 BC). Routledge.
Carandini, A. (2011). Rome: Day One. Princeton University Press.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 753 BCE date is Varro's calculation, not a contemporary record. Archaeological evidence supports Iron Age settlement consolidation in the mid-8th century BCE but does not confirm a single founding event.

location_name: Carthage (Qart-hadašt), Gulf of Tunis, North Africa
main_actor: Phoenician colonists from Tyre, traditionally led by Queen Elissa (Dido)
description: Carthage ('New City' in Phoenician, Qart-hadašt) was founded by Tyrian colonists on the coast of modern Tunisia. The traditional founding date of 814 BCE derives from Timaeus of Tauromenium (via Pompeius Trogus/Justin), correlated with the reign of Pygmalion of Tyre. The legendary founder Elissa (Dido in Virgil's Aeneid) is attested in multiple independent traditions. Archaeological evidence from the Tophet and earliest occupation levels broadly supports a late 9th or early 8th century BCE date. Carthage grew to become the dominant maritime power of the western Mediterranean, controlling trade routes and establishing its own colonial network across North Africa, Sardinia, western Sicily, and Iberia.
silence_reason: Carthaginian archives and literary works were almost entirely destroyed when Rome razed the city in 146 BCE. Carthaginian history is reconstructed primarily from Greek and Roman sources hostile to Carthage. The Phoenician/Punic perspective on Carthage's own history is largely inaccessible.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Carthaginian history is known almost exclusively through its enemies' accounts (Greek and Roman). The Tophet (child burial precinct) has generated intense scholarly debate about whether Carthaginians practiced child sacrifice (as Greek and Roman sources claim) or whether the Tophet was a cemetery for children who died naturally. The destruction of Carthaginian literary culture by Rome in 146 BCE represents one of antiquity's greatest losses of knowledge.

entity_links.notes: Founding of Carthage as a Tyrian colony

sources.citation: Aubet, M.E. (2001). The Phoenicians and the West: Politics, Colonies and Trade. 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press.
Lancel, S. (1995). Carthage: A History. Blackwell.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 814 BCE date is a later Greek calculation. Archaeological evidence supports founding in the late 9th or early 8th century BCE. Margin of error +/- 50 years.

description: The Assyrian siege and destruction of Lachish, the second most important city of the Kingdom of Judah, was a major episode in Sennacherib's campaign against Hezekiah of Judah in 701 BCE. The siege is documented from both sides: Sennacherib commemorated the conquest in elaborate stone reliefs at his palace in Nineveh (now in the British Museum), depicting siege ramps, battering rams, deportation of captives, and impalement of defenders. Archaeological excavations at Tell ed-Duweir confirmed the massive Assyrian siege ramp (the earliest archaeologically attested siege ramp), arrowheads, sling stones, and a mass burial of approximately 1,500 individuals in a nearby cave. The Assyrians did not capture Jerusalem itself, an event attributed to divine intervention in 2 Kings 19.
casualties_source: Mass burial at Lachish: ~1,500 individuals (Ussishkin, 2004). Total military and civilian casualties including deportees unknown.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Lachish reliefs at Nineveh are Assyrian imperial propaganda celebrating the destruction of a city and the deportation of its population. They depict impalement of defenders and forced marches of families into exile. These images document state-sponsored terror as a deliberate military strategy. The Judean perspective (2 Kings 18-19, Isaiah 36-37) frames the same campaign through a theological lens. Both perspectives are partial.

sources.citation: Ussishkin, D. (2004). The Renewed Archaeological Excavations at Lachish (1973-1994). Tel Aviv University, Institute of Archaeology.
Sennacherib, Lachish reliefs, Room XXXVI, Southwest Palace, Nineveh (British Museum, WA 124904-124915). Primary Assyrian visual record, ~690 BCE.
Tanakh, 2 Kings 18:13-19:37; Isaiah 36-37.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The date 701 BCE is well-established through correlation of Assyrian eponym lists, the Taylor Prism, and biblical chronology. This is one of the best-dated events in ancient Near Eastern history.

location_name: Western Mediterranean (Gadir/Cadiz, Sardinia, Sicily, North Africa)
main_actor: Phoenician city-states, primarily Tyre and Sidon
description: Phoenician colonization of the western Mediterranean established a network of trading posts and settlements stretching from the Levant to the Atlantic coast of Iberia. Key foundations include Gadir (Cadiz, traditionally ~1104 BCE but archaeologically ~800 BCE), Motya in Sicily, Sulcis and Nora in Sardinia, and Lixus and Utica in North Africa. The Phoenicians were driven by demand for metals (silver from Iberia, tin from Atlantic routes, copper from Sardinia) and created the first pan-Mediterranean commercial network. They adapted the alphabet (invented ~1050 BCE at Byblos) for commercial record-keeping, and this script was adopted and modified by Greeks, Etruscans, and ultimately Romans to produce the Latin alphabet used today.
silence_reason: Phoenician literary culture is almost entirely lost. No Phoenician historical narratives survive. The colonization is reconstructed from archaeological evidence, Greek literary sources (Herodotus, Thucydides), and scattered Punic inscriptions. The Phoenician perspective on their own expansion is inaccessible.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Phoenician colonization involved displacement of or interaction with indigenous populations (Nuragic Sardinians, Elymians/Sicans in Sicily, Iberians in Spain). These indigenous perspectives are largely unrecoverable. The term 'colonization' should not be anachronistically equated with modern European colonialism, but the power dynamics of commercial expansion and settlement on others' land were real.

sources.citation: Aubet, M.E. (2001). The Phoenicians and the West: Politics, Colonies and Trade. 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press.
Markoe, G.E. (2000). Phoenicians. University of California Press.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 900-700 BCE range covers the main phase of Phoenician western expansion. Traditional literary dates for specific foundations (e.g. Gadir ~1104 BCE from Velleius Paterculus) are centuries earlier than archaeological evidence. Margin of error +/- 100 years for the process as a whole.

main_actor: Quanrong (western nomadic peoples) and disaffected Zhou vassals
description: The sack of the Western Zhou capital Haojing by Quanrong nomads and rebellious vassal lords led by the Marquis of Shen ended the Western Zhou dynasty and began the Eastern Zhou period. King You of Zhou had alienated vassals by deposing his queen (daughter of the Marquis of Shen) in favour of his concubine Bao Si. The Marquis of Shen allied with the Quanrong to attack the capital. King You was killed, and his successor King Ping relocated the capital east to Luoyi (Luoyang), marking the beginning of the Spring and Autumn period (771-476 BCE). The move east drastically reduced the Zhou king's actual power, reducing him to a ceremonial figurehead while vassal states became effectively independent.
ethical_notes: The traditional account (Sima Qian, Shiji) frames the fall of Western Zhou through the lens of female-caused disaster (Bao Si's alleged manipulation of King You). This misogynistic trope of 'femme fatale' causing state collapse is a recurrent motif in Chinese historiography (Daji/Shang, Moxi/Xia) and should be identified as a literary convention rather than historical causation. The actual causes were structural: overextension, nomadic pressure, and vassal autonomy.

entity_links.notes: Western Zhou ends; Eastern Zhou begins as a weakened continuation

sources.citation: Shaughnessy, E.L. (1999). 'Western Zhou History.' In Loewe, M. & Shaughnessy, E.L. (eds.), The Cambridge History of Ancient China. Cambridge University Press.
Sima Qian, Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), 'Zhou Benji' (Basic Annals of Zhou). Primary Chinese historiographic source, ~90 BCE.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The date 771 BCE is well-established in Chinese chronological tradition through the Bamboo Annals and Shiji. It marks the beginning of the Spring and Autumn period and is one of the earliest secure dates in Chinese history.

description: Solon was appointed archon with extraordinary powers to resolve Athens' severe social and economic crisis. His reforms (seisachtheia, 'shaking off of burdens') cancelled existing debts, freed Athenians who had been enslaved for debt, banned future debt-slavery, and reorganised the citizen body into four property classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) with graded political rights. He also reformed weights, measures, and coinage, and encouraged olive oil export. Solon's reforms did not create democracy (that came with Cleisthenes in 508 BCE), but they dismantled the worst abuses of aristocratic power and created the social preconditions for later democratic development. After enacting his laws, Solon reportedly left Athens for ten years to prevent himself from being pressured to modify them.
ethical_notes: Solon's reforms, while progressive for their time, maintained a property-based political hierarchy. The lowest class (thetes) could attend the assembly but not hold office. Women, enslaved people, and non-Athenians were entirely excluded. The seisachtheia freed Athenian debt-slaves but did not abolish chattel slavery of non-Athenians. Solon's surviving poetry (fragments) provides a rare first-person voice from an ancient reformer but is also self-justificatory.

sources.citation: Aristotle, Athenaion Politeia (Constitution of Athens), chs. 5-12. Trans. P.J. Rhodes (1984), Penguin.
Blok, J.H. & Lardinois, A.P.M.H. (eds.) (2006). Solon of Athens: New Historical and Philological Approaches. Brill.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Solon's archonship is conventionally dated to 594/3 BCE based on Athenian archon lists. The date is widely accepted but depends on later chronographic traditions.

description: Peisistratos established himself as tyrant of Athens after three attempts (in 561, 556, and finally 546 BCE, when he returned from exile with mercenaries and Thracian gold). His rule (546-528 BCE) is paradoxically remembered as a period of cultural flourishing and moderate governance. He maintained Solon's constitutional framework while controlling it through clientage and strategic appointment of allies. He commissioned major building projects (the Olympieion temple, improvements to the Agora), organized the Great Panathenaea festival, and may have ordered the first authoritative compilation of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey. His sons Hippias and Hipparchus succeeded him, but Hipparchus was assassinated in 514 BCE and Hippias was expelled in 510 BCE, leading to Cleisthenes' democratic reforms.
ethical_notes: The Greek concept of tyrannos did not originally carry the negative connotation of modern 'tyrant'; it meant an unconstitutional sole ruler. Peisistratos seized power through force and maintained it through a combination of populist policies and coercion. Later Athenian democratic ideology demonized tyranny, making objective assessment of Peisistratos' rule difficult. His patronage of culture served political purposes: creating Athenian civic identity and legitimizing his regime.

sources.citation: Aristotle, Athenaion Politeia (Constitution of Athens), chs. 13-19.
Lavelle, B.M. (2005). Fame, Money, and Power: The Rise of Peisistratos and 'Democratic' Tyranny at Athens. University of Michigan Press.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The date 546 BCE for Peisistratos' final seizure of power is based on Athenian archon lists and Herodotus 1.59-64. His death in 528/7 BCE is also conventionally dated.

main_actor: Judean priestly and scribal circles (Deuteronomist school, Priestly source)
description: The main compositional and editorial phases of the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh) occurred during the late 7th through 5th centuries BCE, encompassing the Josianic reforms (~621 BCE, discovery of the 'Book of the Law' in the Temple, likely an early form of Deuteronomy), the Babylonian Exile (586-539 BCE), and the early Persian period. The Deuteronomistic History (Joshua through 2 Kings) was compiled to explain the destruction of Jerusalem as divine punishment. The Priestly source (P) edited and framed the Pentateuch during or after the Exile. The prophetic books of Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Deutero-Isaiah were composed or edited during this period. This represents one of the most consequential intellectual achievements in human history, producing texts that would become foundational to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Documentary Hypothesis and its successors (Wellhausen, Friedman, Baden) are scholarly constructs, not proven facts. Dating biblical texts is contentious because it intersects with religious belief. Some traditions hold Mosaic authorship of the Torah (~13th century BCE). AtlasPI follows the scholarly consensus on compositional dating without dismissing alternative traditions. The Hebrew Bible's narratives of conquest (Joshua) and ethnic exclusivism (Ezra-Nehemiah) must be understood in their historical context without either endorsing or anachronistically condemning them.

sources.citation: Friedman, R.E. (1987). Who Wrote the Bible? Harper & Row.
Carr, D.M. (2011). The Formation of the Hebrew Bible: A New Reconstruction. Oxford University Press.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The dating of biblical composition is approximate and debated. The range 620-500 BCE covers the main editorial activity from Josiah's reform through the early Persian period. Individual texts have their own complex compositional histories spanning longer periods.

location_name: Pontic-Caspian steppe (modern Ukraine, southern Russia, Central Asia)
description: The Scythians, Iranian-speaking horse nomads, dominated the Pontic-Caspian steppe from approximately the 8th to 3rd centuries BCE. They displaced the Cimmerians westward and established a vast territory stretching from the Danube to the borders of China. Scythian military power was based on mounted archery and extreme mobility. They raided the Assyrian Empire, invaded the Near East (~630s BCE, reaching the borders of Egypt), and successfully resisted the Persian invasion of Darius I (~513 BCE) through scorched-earth tactics. The Scythian royal burials (kurgans) at sites like Arzhan, Pazyryk, and Tolstaya Mogila reveal extraordinary artistic sophistication (the 'Animal Style'), advanced metallurgy, and long-distance trade connections.
silence_reason: The Scythians left no written records. All textual accounts come from settled civilizations they interacted with (Herodotus, Assyrian annals, Chinese sources on the Saka). Scythian history is reconstructed from archaeology and hostile external accounts.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Herodotus (Book IV) is our primary literary source but describes the Scythians through a Greek lens, emphasizing 'barbaric' customs (scalping, cannabis use, human sacrifice at royal funerals). Archaeological evidence confirms some of these practices but also reveals a sophisticated culture. The modern-day political appropriation of Scythian identity by Ukrainian and Russian nationalist movements adds a contemporary dimension to the representation of this culture.

sources.citation: Cunliffe, B. (2019). The Scythians: Nomad Warriors of the Steppe. Oxford University Press.
Herodotus, Histories, Book IV. Trans. A. de Selincourt (1954), Penguin.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 750-500 BCE range covers the main phase of Scythian expansion and dominance on the Pontic steppe. Scythian presence in the region began earlier and continued later; these dates represent the peak. Margin of error +/- 50 years.

main_actor: Nok culture communities of the Jos Plateau and surrounding regions
description: The Nok culture of central Nigeria produced the earliest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa, alongside evidence of early iron smelting dating to at least 500 BCE. The distinctive terracotta figures, with their triangular eyes, elaborate hairstyles, and expressive postures, represent a sophisticated artistic tradition. Nok ironworking is significant because it appears to have developed independently rather than diffusing from the Near East or North Africa, challenging diffusionist models that denied indigenous African technological innovation. The culture's economy combined agriculture (oil palm, cowpea), iron production, and possibly tin mining. The Nok culture disappeared by approximately 200 CE for unknown reasons.
silence_reason: The Nok culture left no written records and no oral traditions directly attributable to it survive. All knowledge comes from archaeological excavation, primarily of terracotta fragments and iron-smelting furnaces. The culture's language, political organization, and self-designation are unknown.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Nok culture has been devastated by looting since the 1990s. Thousands of terracotta figures have been illegally excavated and sold on the international art market, destroying irreplaceable archaeological context. Nigeria has pursued repatriation of looted Nok pieces from European collections. The independent development of iron smelting in West Africa challenges long-standing diffusionist assumptions that all technology originated in the Near East and spread outward.

sources.citation: Breunig, P. (ed.) (2014). Nok: African Sculpture in Archaeological Context. Africa Magna Verlag.
Holl, A.F.C. (2009). 'Early West African Metallurgies: New Data and Old Orthodoxy.' Journal of World Prehistory 22(4), 415-438.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Dates based on thermoluminescence and radiocarbon dating of terracotta and associated iron-smelting contexts. The ~500 BCE date is for the earliest well-attested phase; some dates for iron smelting in the region extend back to ~900 BCE but are debated. Margin of error +/- 200 years.

location_name: San Lorenzo / La Venta, Gulf Coast, Mesoamerica
main_actor: Olmec civilization populations of the Gulf lowlands
description: The Olmec civilization, often called Mesoamerica's 'mother culture,' experienced its final decline between approximately 400-350 BCE with the abandonment of its last major centre at La Venta. The Olmec had flourished from approximately 1500 BCE, producing colossal basalt heads (up to 3.4 metres tall and 50 tonnes), developing early Mesoamerican writing and calendrical systems, and establishing long-distance trade networks for jade, obsidian, and magnetite. San Lorenzo was destroyed and abandoned ~900 BCE; La Venta flourished until ~400 BCE. The causes of the final collapse remain unclear: volcanic activity, environmental change, and internal social upheaval have all been proposed. Olmec cultural influence persisted in successor civilizations (Zapotec, Maya, Teotihuacan).
silence_reason: Olmec writing (if it constitutes a full script) remains largely undeciphered. The civilization's own name is unknown; 'Olmec' is a later Aztec term meaning 'rubber people.' Internal historical narratives are inaccessible.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The term 'mother culture' for the Olmec has been challenged by scholars who argue that Mesoamerican civilization arose through multiple independent developments rather than diffusing from a single source (the 'sister culture' model). The colossal heads' African-like facial features have been exploited by Afrocentric theories claiming African colonization of Mesoamerica, a claim rejected by mainstream archaeology and by Indigenous American scholars as denying indigenous agency.

sources.citation: Pool, C.A. (2007). Olmec Archaeology and Early Mesoamerica. Cambridge University Press.
Diehl, R.A. (2004). The Olmecs: America's First Civilization. Thames & Hudson.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The ~400 BCE date for the end of La Venta is based on radiocarbon dating and ceramic sequences. The Olmec decline was gradual, not a single event. Margin of error +/- 100 years.

main_actor: Neo-Elamite ruling dynasties of southwestern Iran
description: The Neo-Elamite period (c. 1000-539 BCE) represents the final centuries of independent Elamite civilization in southwestern Iran before its absorption into the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Following the destruction of Susa by Ashurbanipal of Assyria in 647/646 BCE, the Elamite polity fragmented but reconstituted in reduced form. The Neo-Elamite kingdoms maintained the Elamite language and cultural traditions alongside increasing Iranian (Persian) settlement in the Anshan highlands. The region's strategic importance (controlling passes between Mesopotamia and the Iranian plateau) made it contested territory between Assyria, Babylon, and the emerging Median and Persian powers. The Achaemenid Persians, who originated as vassals in Anshan, eventually absorbed Elam, with Susa becoming a major Persian imperial capital.
silence_reason: Neo-Elamite written sources are extremely scarce. The Elamite language in this period is poorly attested. Most information comes from Assyrian and Babylonian references to Elam. The internal history, dynastic sequences, and political structures of Neo-Elamite kingdoms are fragmentary.
ethical_notes: Elamite civilization, one of the oldest in the world (predating Mesopotamian urbanism at some sites), is systematically underrepresented in world history education, which typically presents Mesopotamia and Egypt as the only 'cradles of civilization.' The absorption of Elam into the Persian Empire represents a case of cultural displacement where the conquered civilization's contribution (including the Elamite language's continued use in Persian imperial administration) is obscured.

sources.citation: Potts, D.T. (1999). The Archaeology of Elam: Formation and Transformation of an Ancient Iranian State. Cambridge University Press.
Henkelman, W.F.M. (2008). The Other Gods Who Are: Studies in Elamite-Iranian Acculturation Based on the Persepolis Fortification Texts. Nederlands Instituut voor het Nabije Oosten.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The Neo-Elamite period is poorly dated; the 1000-539 BCE range follows Potts' periodization. The end date corresponds to the fall of Babylon to Cyrus (after which Elam was fully within the Achaemenid sphere). Margin of error +/- 50 years for sub-periods.

location_name: Ecbatana (Hagmatana, modern Hamadan), Media, western Iran
description: The Median kingdom emerged in western Iran during the 7th century BCE as Iranian-speaking tribes consolidated under a unified leadership. Herodotus attributes the foundation to Deioces (Daiaukku in Assyrian sources), who allegedly unified the Medes and built the capital Ecbatana with its seven concentric walls. The historical accuracy of Herodotus' account is debated, but Assyrian records confirm the existence of Median polities from at least the 9th century BCE. Under Cyaxares (625-585 BCE), Media became a major power: allied with Babylon, the Medes destroyed the Assyrian Empire (capturing Nineveh in 612 BCE) and controlled a vast territory from Anatolia to eastern Iran. The Median kingdom was overthrown by its Persian vassal Cyrus the Great in 550 BCE.
silence_reason: No Median written records have been found. The Medes are known only through Assyrian, Babylonian, and Greek sources (especially Herodotus). Whether a centralized 'Median Empire' existed (as Herodotus describes) or whether the Medes formed a looser confederation is vigorously debated. Archaeological identification of specifically 'Median' material culture remains controversial.
ethical_notes: The historical existence and nature of the Median kingdom is one of the most debated questions in ancient Near Eastern history. Some scholars (e.g., Sancisi-Weerdenburg) have argued that the 'Median Empire' is largely a Greek literary construction. Others maintain that Assyrian and Babylonian evidence supports a significant Median polity. AtlasPI records the event with appropriate uncertainty.

sources.citation: Sancisi-Weerdenburg, H. (1988). 'Was There Ever a Median Empire?' In Kuhrt, A. & Sancisi-Weerdenburg, H. (eds.), Achaemenid History III. Leiden.
Herodotus, Histories, Book I.96-106. Trans. A. de Selincourt (1954), Penguin.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 678 BCE date for Deioces follows Herodotus' chronology, which is disputed. Assyrian sources reference 'Daiaukku' in 715 BCE but it is uncertain whether this is the same figure. The consolidation of Median power was a process rather than a single event.

description: The Spartan constitution traditionally attributed to the legendary lawgiver Lycurgus transformed Sparta into a militarised society unique in the Greek world. The system included: the agoge (rigorous state education for boys from age 7), common messes (syssitia), land redistribution, prohibition of gold and silver currency, a dual monarchy, a council of elders (gerousia), and the assembly (apella). Whether Lycurgus was a historical figure is debated; the reforms likely developed gradually over the 8th-7th centuries BCE rather than being enacted at once. The system depended entirely on the helot population (subjugated Messenians and Laconians) who worked the land as state-owned serfs, supporting the Spartan citizen elite (homoioi, 'equals') in their full-time military lifestyle.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Spartan system was built on the systematic oppression of the helot population, who were subjected to annual ritual declarations of war (krypteia), routine violence, and permanent servitude. The 'Spartan ideal' of discipline and equality among citizens depended entirely on the unfreedom of the vast majority of the population. Western admiration for Sparta (from Plutarch through 18th-century republicans to modern popular culture) typically romanticizes the citizen-warriors while erasing the helots. The helots' perspective is entirely absent from surviving sources.

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus. (Loeb Classical Library edition).
Hodkinson, S. (2000). Property and Wealth in Classical Sparta. Duckworth.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Lycurgus is traditionally dated to the 9th century BCE, but the reforms he is credited with were likely implemented gradually during the 8th-7th centuries. The ~700 BCE date represents an approximate mid-point. No contemporary records exist.

location_name: Eastern Iran or Central Asia (exact location debated)
description: Zarathustra (Zoroaster in Greek) composed the Gathas, the oldest and most sacred hymns of the Avesta, founding what became the Zoroastrian religion. The Gathas present a cosmic dualism between Ahura Mazda (Wise Lord) and Angra Mainyu (Destructive Spirit), concepts of individual moral choice, judgment after death, and eschatological renewal of the world. These ideas profoundly influenced later Judaism (during and after the Babylonian Exile), Christianity, and Islam. The dating of Zarathustra is one of the most contested questions in Iranian studies: Greek tradition placed him 258 years before Alexander (thus ~588 BCE), while some scholars argue for a much earlier date (1500-1200 BCE) based on linguistic analysis of the Gathas' archaic Avestan language.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The dating and historicity of Zarathustra are genuinely uncertain, and AtlasPI must not present contested scholarly opinions as established fact. The Zoroastrian community's own traditional dating differs from academic consensus. The influence of Zoroastrian concepts (dualism, eschatology, angelology) on Abrahamic religions is significant but debated in its extent. Zoroastrianism was persecuted after the Arab conquest of Iran (7th century CE), and presenting its origins accurately honours a living religious tradition that has suffered historical suppression.

sources.citation: Boyce, M. (1975). A History of Zoroastrianism, Vol. I: The Early Period. Brill.
Gathas of Zarathustra, Yasna 28-34, 43-51, 53 (Avesta). Primary sacred text.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 650-550 BCE range follows the 'traditional' Greek dating (Boyce, Gnoli). The 'high' linguistic dating places Zarathustra in the 2nd millennium BCE. The uncertainty is extreme and inherent to the evidence. The Zoroastrian calendar (Qadimi/Shenshai/Fasli) is not relevant for BCE dating.

main_actor: Multiple Mediterranean and Near Eastern societies
description: The transition from bronze to iron as the primary metal for tools and weapons was completed across the Near East and Mediterranean during the 8th-7th centuries BCE. While meteoric iron was used occasionally from the 3rd millennium BCE and smelted iron appeared in Anatolia by ~1200 BCE, the widespread adoption of iron technology accelerated after the Late Bronze Age Collapse disrupted tin trade routes essential for bronze production. Iron ore was far more abundant than tin, making iron tools and weapons accessible to a broader population. The technology spread unevenly: sub-Saharan Africa developed independent iron smelting (possibly by 1000-800 BCE in the Great Lakes region and West Africa), and iron reached China through steppe trade routes by ~600 BCE. The Iron Age transformation had profound social consequences: iron plows increased agricultural productivity, iron weapons democratized military capability beyond chariot-owning aristocracies.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The diffusionist model of iron technology spreading from a single Anatolian origin is increasingly challenged by evidence of independent iron smelting in Africa (Holl 2009, Killick 2015). Presenting the Near East as the sole source of ironworking perpetuates a Eurasian-centric technological narrative. Multiple independent origins must be acknowledged.

sources.citation: Pleiner, R. (2000). Iron in Archaeology: The European Bloomery Smelters. Archeologicky Ustav AVCR.
Killick, D. (2015). 'Invention and Innovation in African Iron-smelting Technologies.' Cambridge Archaeological Journal 25(1), 307-319.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 700-600 BCE range represents the period when iron became dominant over bronze in the Near East and Mediterranean. The transition was gradual and regional; individual sites and regions adopted iron at different times. Margin of error +/- 100 years.
